items.mistake: Saying 'there is a 95% probability the true mean is in [114, 126]'
items.fix: Say 'we used a 95% confidence procedure; if we repeated sampling, about 95% of such intervals would contain the true mean' or 'the interval is consistent with means from about 95% of repeated samples.'
items.why: After data are observed, the interval is fixed; the parameter is not random in the frequentist framework. Probability applies to the procedure, not to θ given this interval.
items.example: Dashboard label: `95% CI for mean latency: [114, 126] ms (frequentist procedure, not P(true mean ∈ interval | data) = 0.95)`.

items.mistake: Using the z formula with s/√n when n is small without checking t critical values
items.fix: When σ is unknown and n is modest, use t_{α/2, n−1} instead of z_{α/2}; rely on z only for large n or when σ is genuinely known.
items.why: Replacing σ with s adds extra uncertainty; the t distribution accounts for that. Using z when n is small yields intervals that are too narrow.
items.example: n = 15, x̄ = 10, s = 4: t_{0.025, 14} ≈ 2.14, not 1.96 → CI is about 9% wider than the naive z interval.

items.mistake: Applying the independent-sample proportion CI to clustered or sequential data
items.fix: Use cluster-robust, blocked, or time-series-aware methods when observations are correlated; bootstrap with the correct resampling unit if needed.
items.why: Standard SE formulas assume independent observations; positive correlation inflates effective variance and makes nominal 95% CIs undercover.
items.example: 100 prompts × 10 repeated generations each are not 1000 i.i.d. trials—resample at the prompt level, not the generation level.
